posts.title: Guide day
posts.caption:
My first day fishing in Belize started the way a lot of good fishing days probably do ГғВғГӮВўГғВӮГӮВҖГғВӮГӮВ” with the plan changing before it even began.

A storm rolled through in the morning and pushed our pickup back, but we still made it happen. By the time Eduardo, my guide, and I got moving, the weather was still hanging around. The air was thick, heavy, and wet. The humidity here is no joke. Running to the first spot during the tail end of the storm felt like breathing through a bandana.

We started the day cruising through the mangroves, which honestly would have been worth it even without a fish. We saw some tarpon rolling and for a second it felt like the day might start with chaos. But they never gave us the shot we needed, and we didnГғВғГӮВўГғВӮГӮВҖГғВӮГӮВҷt hook up.

Once the storm cleared, the day opened up. The light got better, the flats started to show themselves, and suddenly we could actually see fish.

I had been hoping for a shot at my first bonefish, and we got it. Then another. Then another. By the end of the day, Eduardo and I had netted three bones. They werenГғВғГӮВўГғВӮГӮВҖГғВӮГӮВҷt easy fish either. Today everything felt spooky. Every push of water, every bad cast, every little mistake seemed to matter. These fish were hard to approach and gave very little room for error. 

I had one shot at a permit, but my double haul into the wind needs some elbow grease. Tomorrow IГғВғГӮВўГғВӮГӮВҖГғВӮГӮВҷll come with lighter tippet and more knowledge!

posts.title: Good Morning!
posts.caption:
I canГўВҖВҷt emphasize enough how humid it is here.

Fly fishing gear that would reliably dry out in less than ten minutes in the Rocky Mountains was still wet after hanging up all night. Everything holds moisture here. My hat was damp from sitting on the ground. Nothing really dries.

Secret Beach is off grid, the energy comes from solar, and the water is captured rainwater. It makes you get the rhythm of the place. The lifestyle really only works while the sun is up. Once the light starts fading, everything begins to slow down.

It gets quiet here about 45 minutes before sunset, around 6 p.m., because thatГўВҖВҷs about how long it takes to golf cart back to San Pedro. The whole place has this built-in curfew. Not because anyone tells you to leave, but because the island, the roads, and the lack of light kind of make the decision for you.

That off-grid rhythm makes for early nights and early mornings.

This morning I was out fishing at 5 a.m. And yes, Mom, I got enough sleep. I was in bed by 9:30.

The flats were still rough from the storm thatГўВҖВҷs been hovering close by, but today should be the last day. The fish were still not easy. I had a few shots at bonefish, but they are unbelievably spooky. A bad step, a cast landing a little too heavy, the fly line catching the wrong light, they gone.

I didnГўВҖВҷt get one to eat this morning, but I did get a barracuda to crush a shrimp fly, which was not exactly what I was expecting. Somehow it didnГўВҖВҷt tear off the tippet or destroy the fly, which feels like a small miracle.

Barracuda are pure predators. They seem to appear out of nowhere, like they were never there and then suddenly theyГўВҖВҷre gone just as quick as they showed up. No hesitation, no delicate inspection, no slow approach. Just violence.

It was a short morning, but a good one. Humid, quiet, early, and full of those little moments that make fishing here feel different from anywhere else IГўВҖВҷve been.
